1.synthesis:
Amaziah son of Joash becomes king of Judah in the second year of Jehoash of Israel. The theological evaluation is qualified: he did right in the sight of the Lord, yet not like David his father — the high places were not removed and the people still sacrificed and burned incense there. His first act of justice is executing the servants who assassinated his father Joash, but he does not execute their children — a deliberate obedience to the Mosaic principle of individual responsibility in Deut 24:16. Militarily he defeats ten thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt and seizes Sela, renaming it Joktheel. Flushed with success, he challenges Jehoash of Israel to battle — a challenge Jehoash answers with a contemptuous parable about the thistle and the cedar. Jehoash warns that the glory of defeating Edom has made Amaziah overconfident; he should stay home lest a misfortune befall both himself and Judah. Amaziah does not listen, Judah is routed at Beth Shemesh, Amaziah is captured, and Jehoash breaks down four hundred cubits of Jerusalem's wall and takes gold, silver, hostages, and returns to Samaria. Amaziah outlives Jehoash by fifteen years but eventually faces a conspiracy; he flees to Lachish where he is killed. The people of Judah then take his sixteen-year-old son Azariah and make him king.

John Gill marks the Deuteronomy 24 reference as deliberate: Amaziah's restraint in executing only the murderers, not their sons, is a rare instance of a king explicitly following the Mosaic code in a matter of justice rather than convenience. Matthew Henry observes that Amaziah's defeat by Jehoash is the classic consequence of pride following victory — success over a lesser enemy inflates confidence for a battle against a greater opponent that has not been divinely commanded. Keil and Delitzsch note that Jehoash's parable (thistle asking for cedar's daughter in marriage) is the sharpest literary moment of these chapters: a northern king demonstrates more wisdom about limits than a southern king with a better covenant heritage.
1.historical_context: Amaziah's reign (ca. 796–767 BC) spans a period of significant regional flux. The Valley of Salt victory over Edom recovers territory and influence that Judah had lost. Sela, renamed Joktheel, has been identified with the site later known as Petra. The conflict with Israel at Beth Shemesh is historically significant: Judah's defeat by Israel at a Judahite site reflects the power disparity between the two kingdoms during Jehoash's expansionist period. The breach of Jerusalem's north wall — from the Ephraim Gate to the Corner Gate, four hundred cubits — represents a deliberate humiliation rather than a full conquest. The conspiracies against both Amaziah (14:19) and his son Azariah (15:10) suggest ongoing instability in the Davidic court despite the relative prosperity of Uzziah's long reign.
1.literary_structure: Amaziah's reign is structured around two military campaigns: the Edom victory (14:7) that produces pride, and the Israel defeat (14:8–14) that is pride's consequence. The parable Jehoash offers (14:9) is the literary centerpiece — wisdom literature embedded in historical narrative. Amaziah's death by conspiracy closes the narrative, and the installation of Azariah by the people of Judah (14:21) bridges to the next reign.
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry's reading of Amaziah's defeat identifies it as a case study in unchecked success: "prosperity has been the ruin of many." Amaziah's obedience to the Deuteronomy principle in the assassination case is genuine; his subsequent overreach against Israel is not divinely commanded and is explicitly warned against by Jehoash. The lesson is not that victory is sinful but that every victory must be processed with renewed humility before God rather than as fuel for the next venture. Calvin observes that the refusal to heed the wise counsel of Jehoash — a warning from an unexpected source, an Israelite king — is itself a failure: God sometimes delivers wisdom through unlikely instruments, and the proud heart that refuses it from such sources pays the price of its own overconfidence.
1.christology: Amaziah's reign that is "right but not like David" captures the insufficiency of every human approximation of ideal kingship. The qualified evaluation — genuine obedience in some matters, persistent failure in others — is the pattern of all Davidic kings short of the final Son of David. Christ alone fulfills the David standard unconditionally: in him there is no qualification, no "but the high places were not removed." The breach in Jerusalem's wall under Amaziah's foolishness will be repaired by Uzziah and later by Hezekiah; it points forward to the rebuilding of the walls of God's community that Christ undertakes, establishing the church whose gates even death cannot breach (Matt 16:18).

23.synthesis:
Jeroboam son of Jehoash becomes king in Samaria in the fifteenth year of Amaziah and reigns forty-one years — the longest reign of any northern king. The evaluation is negative in the standard formula: he does evil in the sight of the Lord, not departing from the sins of Jeroboam son of Nebat. Yet what follows is remarkable: the Lord observes that Israel's affliction was very bitter, and there was no one, whether bond or free, to help Israel. He had not said he would blot out the name of Israel from under heaven, so he saved them by the hand of Jeroboam son of Jehoash. Jeroboam restores Israel's territory from the entrance of Hamath to the Sea of the Arabah — the approximate boundaries of the Solomonic empire — in fulfillment of the word of the Lord spoken through Jonah son of Amittai the prophet from Gath Hepher. Jeroboam's reign is the high-water mark of northern kingdom prosperity and territorial extent.

John Gill is struck by the contradiction at the heart of this summary: a king who does evil saves Israel by divine appointment. God's compassion on Israel's affliction, not Jeroboam's personal righteousness, is the engine of the deliverance. Matthew Henry observes that this is one of the clearest Old Testament examples of God working through an imperfect and even wicked instrument for the good of his people — not because the instrument deserves it but because God's covenant purposes for Israel are not contingent on finding perfect human instruments. Keil and Delitzsch note the reference to Jonah son of Amittai is significant: this is the only non-Jonah-book reference to the prophet, and it places his ministry in the context of Israel's territorial restoration rather than Nineveh's repentance — indicating Jonah was active as a prophet of hope before his Nineveh mission.

The reign of Jeroboam II (ca. 793–753 BC) is the setting for the prophetic ministry of Amos and Hosea, both of whom critique the prosperity and injustice of his era even as Kings records only territorial success. The brevity of the Kings account is significant: despite the longest northern reign and the greatest territorial extent, the narrator gives Jeroboam II only seven verses — because his spiritual legacy was null.
23.historical_context: Jeroboam II's reign coincided with Assyrian weakness under Adad-nirari III and his successors, allowing Israel to expand northward (Hamath) and recover Transjordanian territory from the weakened Aramean state. This external context explains why a king evaluated negatively could achieve dramatic territorial success: the geopolitical window was open regardless of Jeroboam's spiritual condition. The prophets Amos (Amos 1:1) and Hosea (Hos 1:1) both begin their prophetic careers during Jeroboam II's reign and document the social injustice and religious corruption that characterized the period despite its outward prosperity — the violence, exploitation of the poor, and hollow liturgy that Jeroboam's expansion had neither addressed nor reduced. Jonah son of Amittai from Gath Hepher (14:25) is thus a prophet of Israel's prosperity whose mission to Nineveh (the book of Jonah) represents a theological expansion beyond national boundaries.
23.literary_structure: The Jeroboam II notice is among the most compact accounts of the longest northern reign. The theological pattern established — evil king used by God for national good because of God's compassion, not the king's merit — is stated explicitly rather than left for the reader to infer. The mention of Jonah provides both prophetic grounding for the territorial expansion and an intertextual link to the book of Jonah, which sits within this historical period. The reign's end — Jeroboam died, Zechariah his son reigned in his place — sets up the rapid dynastic collapse of chapter 15.
23.theological_application: The statement that God saved Israel by Jeroboam's hand because "he had not said he would blot out the name of Israel" is a crucial theological datum: divine mercy operates on the basis of divine covenant faithfulness, not human deserving. Calvin reads this as a reminder that God is not bound to reward only the virtuous — he works through flawed instruments because his purposes are not contingent on human perfection. For the church, this is both comfort and warning: comfort, because God's purposes for his people do not depend on finding perfect leaders; warning, because prosperity under a wicked leader does not indicate divine approval of the leader's character. Amos's oracles against Israel during Jeroboam's prosperous reign make exactly this point.
23.christology: Jeroboam II's reign that restores Solomonic-era boundaries through divine compassion rather than covenant faithfulness is a type of the greater restoration accomplished by Christ. Where Jeroboam restored geography temporarily and imperfectly, Christ restores the full scope of the Abrahamic promise permanently — "all the nations of the earth" blessed in his seed (Gen 22:18). The fact that God acts not because Israel deserves it but because he "had not said he would blot out their name" points to the grace principle: the new covenant is established not because humanity deserves salvation but because God "so loved the world" (John 3:16). Jonah's mention here, just before his book's account of mercy to Nineveh, anticipates the universality of Christ's mission.
